step.title: Step 25
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 16 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 257, level 1.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: GAME OVER triggered when purple reached 1445. Board appears to have reset with green blocks back. Purple bar is smaller. Game may have restarted.
- Plan: Check current state, understand if game reset, try new approach - maybe need to collect blocks quickly before timer runs out.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 256 actions; a strong score needs about 21 or fewer. You are 12.2x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 61 actions returned the board to a state already seen 61 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 61 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so the game over state shows:
- 7 green blocks (same positions as after ACTION7: rows 3 and 15)
- NO purple bar (purple=0 objects)
- Player at (38, 20)
- Sky blue: 2 objects, 1805 pixels (was 1 object, 2056 pixels before)
- White: 1 object, 64 pixels (this is new - the white bar at bottom)
- Yellow: 1 object, 2 pixels (new)
The sky blue area split into 2 objects and has fewer pixels (1805 vs 2056). The purple bar is gone, replaced by a white bar. This looks like a "game over" screen state.
But wait - the game says "game_over" but I can still take actions. Maybe the game allows me to continue after game over, or maybe I need to restart.
Looking at the image, I see the green blocks are still there. The player is in the bottom area. The bottom bar is white now instead of purple.
Wait, I notice something important: the sky blue area has 2 objects now (was 1 before) with 1805 pixels (was 2056). This means the game over state changed the board. The sky blue area split.
But the key question is: can I still play? The valid actions are still LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7. Let me try to continue playing and see if the level can still be completed.
Actually, let me reconsider the entire problem. I've been treating this as a game where I need to collect blocks, but maybe the goal is completely different. Let me think about what a 21-action solution could look like:
1. If the solution is just collecting 7 blocks: 7 MOUSE actions = 7 actions. But the level didn't complete after collecting 7 blocks initially.
2. If the solution is collecting 7 blocks + 7 ACTION7 + 7 more blocks = 21 actions. But I tried this and it didn't complete.
3. If the solution involves player movement + collecting: maybe 7 moves + 7 collects + 7 ACTION7 = 21 actions.
Wait, I just realized something. The game says "game_over" but I'm still on level 1. Maybe "game_over" means the level failed and I need to restart. But there's no "restart" action in the valid actions list.
Let me try something: what if I just collect the 7 green blocks and the level completes? The game might have reset to a state where collecting all 7 blocks completes the level.
Actually, looking more carefully at the state: the sky blue area has 2 objects now. This is different from the initial state where it was 1 object. So the game didn't fully reset. The game over state is a modified version of the board.
Let me try collecting the 7 green blocks and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Game over state - purple bar gone, replaced by white bar. Sky blue split into 2 objects (1805 px). 7 green blocks present. Player at (38, 20). Can still take actions.
Plan: Try collecting all 7 green blocks - maybe level can still be completed from game over state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Collect all 7 green blocks
seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green blocks: {len(greens)}")

mouse_actions = []
for g in greens:
    boundary = g['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in boundary]
    cols = [p[1] for p in boundary]
    center_row = sum(rows) // len(rows)
    center_col = sum(cols) // len(cols)
    mouse_actions.append({'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': center_row, 'col': center_col})

action(mouse_actions)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print(f"\nAfter collecting 7:")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over', False)}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
print(f"Run complete: {last_action_result.get('run_complete', False)}")

# Check greens remaining
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Remaining greens: {len(greens)}")

# Check sky blue
sky = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
print(f"Sky blue objects: {len(sky)}, total pixels: {sum(n['pixels'] for n in sky)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Green blocks: 7

After collecting 7:
Level completed: False
Game over: False
Done: False
Run complete: False
Remaining greens: 0
Sky blue objects: 1, total pixels: 2056
